documentation:
# Interchange

Interchange is a data type and interchange format library.
It has been primarily developed for the py2neo project.

classes.name: interchange.geo.Point
documentation:
A point within a geometric space. This type is generally used
via its subclasses and should not be instantiated directly unless
there is no subclass defined for the required SRID.

methods.name: interchange.packstream.Unpacker._hydrate_point
documentation:
Create a new instance of a Point subclass from a raw
set of fields. The subclass chosen is determined by the
given SRID; a ValueError will be raised if no such
subclass can be found.

functions.name: interchange.math.nano_add
documentation:
Perform lossless fractional addition,
up to 9 digits of decimal precision.

> ```pycon
> >>> 0.7 + 0.2
> 0.8999999999999999
> >>> -0.7 + 0.2
> -0.49999999999999994
> >>> nano_add(0.7, 0.2)
> 0.9
> >>> nano_add(-0.7, 0.2)
> -0.5
> ```

```
param x



param y



return

```

functions.name: interchange.math.nano_sub
documentation:
Perform lossless fractional subtraction,
up to 9 digits of decimal precision.

> ```pycon
> >>> 0.7 - 0.2
> 0.49999999999999994
> >>> -0.7 - 0.2
> -0.8999999999999999
> >>> nano_sub(0.7, 0.2)
> 0.5
> >>> nano_sub(-0.7, 0.2)
> -0.9
> ```

```
param x



param y



return

```

functions.name: interchange.math.nano_mul
documentation:
Perform lossless fractional multiplication,
up to 9 digits of decimal precision.

> ```pycon
> >>> 0.7 * 0.2
> 0.13999999999999999
> >>> -0.7 * 0.2
> -0.13999999999999999
> >>> nano_mul(0.7, 0.2)
> 0.14
> >>> nano_mul(-0.7, 0.2)
> -0.14
> ```

```
param x



param y



return

```

functions.name: interchange.math.nano_div
documentation:
Perform lossless fractional division,
up to 9 digits of decimal precision.

> ```pycon
> >>> 0.7 / 0.2
> 3.4999999999999996
> >>> -0.7 / 0.2
> -3.4999999999999996
> >>> nano_div(0.7, 0.2)
> 3.5
> >>> nano_div(-0.7, 0.2)
> -3.5
> ```

```
param x



param y



return

```

functions.name: interchange.math.round_half_to_even
documentation:
> ```pycon
> >>> round_half_to_even(3)
> 3
> >>> round_half_to_even(3.2)

> >>> round_half_to_even(3.5)

> >>> round_half_to_even(3.7)

> 4
> >>> round_half_to_even(4.2)
> 4
> >>> round_half_to_even(4.5)
> 4
> >>> round_half_to_even(4.7)

> ```

```
param n



return

```

classes.name: interchange.collections.PropertyDict

methods.name: interchange.collections.PropertyDict.setdefault
documentation:
If *key* is in the PropertyDict, return its value. If not,
insert *key* with a value of *default* and return *default*
unless *default* is `None`, in which case do nothing.

The value of *default* defaults to `None`.

```
param key



param default



return

```

methods.name: interchange.collections.PropertyDict.update
documentation:
Update the PropertyDict with the key-value pairs from
*iterable* followed by the keyword arguments from *kwargs*.
Individual properties already in the PropertyDict will be
overwritten by those in *iterable* and subsequently by those
in *kwargs* if the keys match. Any value of `None` will
effectively delete the property with that key, should it exist.

```
param iterable



param kwargs



return

```

Mutable key-value property store.

A dictionary for property values that treats {const}`None`
and missing values as semantically identical.

PropertyDict instances can be created and used in a similar way
to a standard dictionary. For example:

```
>>> fruit = PropertyDict({"name": "banana", "colour": "yellow"})
>>> fruit["name"]
'banana'
```

The key difference with a PropertyDict is in how it handles
missing values. Instead of raising a {py:class}`KeyError`,
attempts to access a missing value will simply return
{py:const}`None` instead.

These are the operations that the PropertyDict can support:

```
.. describe:: len(d)

    Return the number of items in the PropertyDict `d`.
```

```
.. describe:: d[key]

    Return the item of `d` with key `key`. Returns :py:const:`None`
    if key is not in the map.
```

```
.. describe:: d == other

    Return ``True`` if ``d`` is equal to ``other`` after all
    ``None`` values have been removed from ``other``.
```

```
.. describe:: properties != other

    Return ``True`` if ``d`` is unequal to ``other`` after all
    ``None`` values have been removed from ``other``.
```

```
.. describe:: d[key]

    Return the value of *d* with key *key* or ``None`` if the key
    is missing.
```

```
.. describe:: d[key] = value

    Set the value of *d* with key *key* to *value* or remove the
    item if *value* is ``None``.
```

classes.name: interchange.collections.KeyValueList

methods.name: interchange.collections.KeyValueList.__getitem__
documentation:
Get a single item.

```pycon
>>> kvl = KeyValueList([('red', 'rose'), ('blue', 'sea'),
... ('green', 'grass'), ('blue', 'sky')])
>>> kvl[2]
('green', 'grass')
>>> kvl[9]
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in ?
IndexError: list index out of range
```

methods.name: interchange.collections.KeyValueList.__getslice__
documentation:
Get a slice of items.

```pycon
>>> kvl = KeyValueList([('red', 'rose'), ('blue', 'sea'),
... ('green', 'grass'), ('blue', 'sky')])
>>> kvl[1:3]
KeyValueList([('blue', 'sea'), ('green', 'grass')])
```

methods.name: interchange.collections.KeyValueList.__setitem__
documentation:
Set a single item.

```pycon
>>> kvl = KeyValueList([('red', 'rose'), ('blue', 'sea'),
... ('green', 'grass'), ('blue', 'sky')])
>>> kvl[1] = ('yellow', 'sun')
>>> for item in kvl:
...     print(item)
('red', 'rose')
('yellow', 'sun')
('green', 'grass')
('blue', 'sky')
>>> kvl[9] = ('orange', 'orange')
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in ?
IndexError: list assignment index out of range
```

methods.name: interchange.collections.KeyValueList.__setslice__
documentation:
Set a slice of items.

```pycon
>>> kvl = KeyValueList([('red', 'rose'), ('blue', 'sea'),
... ('green', 'grass'), ('blue', 'sky')])
>>> kvl[1:3] = [('yellow', 'sun'), ('grey', 'stone'), ('red', 'berry')]
>>> for item in kvl:
...     print(item)
('red', 'rose')
('yellow', 'sun')
('grey', 'stone')
('red', 'berry')
('blue', 'sky')
```

methods.name: interchange.collections.KeyValueList.__delitem__
documentation:
Delete a single item.

```pycon
>>> kvl = KeyValueList([('red', 'rose'), ('blue', 'sea'),
... ('green', 'grass'), ('blue', 'sky')])
>>> del kvl[1]
>>> for item in kvl:
...     print(item)
('red', 'rose')
('green', 'grass')
('blue', 'sky')
>>> del kvl[9]
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in ?
IndexError: list assignment index out of range
```

methods.name: interchange.collections.KeyValueList.__delslice__
documentation:
Delete a slice of items.

```pycon
>>> kvl = KeyValueList([('red', 'rose'), ('blue', 'sea'),
... ('green', 'grass'), ('blue', 'sky')])
>>> del kvl[1:3]
>>> for item in kvl:
...     print(item)
('red', 'rose')
('blue', 'sky')
```

methods.name: interchange.collections.KeyValueList.__contains__
documentation:
Check for the presence of a particular key-value pair within this
list. This is equivalent to the `has_item` method.

```pycon
>>> kvl = KeyValueList([('red', 'rose'), ('blue', 'sea'),
... ('green', 'grass'), ('blue', 'sky')])
>>> ('blue', 'sea') in kvl
True
>>> ('yellow', 'sun') in kvl
False
>>> ('purple', 'grape') not in kvl
True
>>> ('red', 'rose') not in kvl
False
```

methods.name: interchange.collections.KeyValueList.__iter__
documentation:
Iterate through all items in this list. This is equivalent to the
`items` method with `collect` set to False.

```pycon
>>> kvl = KeyValueList([('red', 'rose'), ('blue', 'sea'),
... ('green', 'grass'), ('blue', 'sky')])
>>> for key, value in kvl:
...     print("{0} -> {1}".format(key, value))
red -> rose
blue -> sea
green -> grass
blue -> sky
```

methods.name: interchange.collections.KeyValueList.append
documentation:
Append a single key-value pair to the end of this list.

```pycon
>>> kvl = KeyValueList()
>>> kvl.append('one', 'eins')
>>> kvl.append('two', 'zwei')
>>> kvl.append('three', 'drei')
>>> for item in kvl:
...     print(item)
('one', 'eins')
('two', 'zwei')
('three', 'drei')
```

methods.name: interchange.collections.KeyValueList.extend
documentation:
Concatenate two lists by adding a list of extra items to the end
of this list. Each item added must be capable of being unpacked into a
key-value pair.

```pycon
>>> kvl = KeyValueList([('one', 'eins'), ('two', 'zwei')])
>>> kvl.extend([('three', 'drei'), ('four', 'vier')])
>>> for item in kvl:
...     print(item)
('one', 'eins')
('two', 'zwei')
('three', 'drei')
('four', 'vier')
>>> kvl.extend(['five', 'six'])
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in ?
ValueError: KeyValueList items must be pairs
```

methods.name: interchange.collections.KeyValueList.insert
documentation:
Insert a key-value pair at a particular position within the list:

```pycon
>>> kvl = KeyValueList({'three': 'drei'})
>>> kvl.insert(0, 'one', 'eins')
>>> kvl.insert(1, 'two', 'zwei')
>>> for item in kvl:
...     print(item)
('one', 'eins')
('two', 'zwei')
('three', 'drei')
```

methods.name: interchange.collections.KeyValueList.has_item
documentation:
Check for the presence of a particular key-value pair within this
list.

```pycon
>>> kvl = KeyValueList([('red', 'rose'), ('blue', 'sea'),
... ('green', 'grass'), ('blue', 'sky')])
>>> kvl.has_item('green', 'grass')
True
>>> kvl.has_item('pink', 'rose')
False
```

methods.name: interchange.collections.KeyValueList.has_key
documentation:
Check for the presence of a particular key within this list.

```pycon
>>> kvl = KeyValueList([('red', 'rose'), ('blue', 'sea'),
... ('green', 'grass'), ('blue', 'sky')])
>>> kvl.has_key('blue')
True
>>> kvl.has_key('yellow')
False
```

methods.name: interchange.collections.KeyValueList.has_value
documentation:
Check for the presence of a particular value within this list.

```pycon
>>> kvl = KeyValueList([('red', 'rose'), ('blue', 'sea'),
... ('green', 'grass'), ('blue', 'sky')])
>>> kvl.has_value('grass')
True
>>> kvl.has_value('sun')
False
```

methods.name: interchange.collections.KeyValueList.get
documentation:
Iterate through all values associated with the specified key.
Non-existent keys return empty iterators.

```pycon
>>> kvl = KeyValueList([('red', 'rose'), ('blue', 'sea'),
... ('green', 'grass'), ('blue', 'sky')])
>>> for value in kvl.get('blue'):
...     print(value)
sea
sky
>>> for value in kvl.get('yellow'):
...     print(value)
```

methods.name: interchange.collections.KeyValueList.put
documentation:
Replace all values associated with the specified key with the
value arguments provided. If fewer values are specified than currently
exist, remaining items will be removed; if more items are specified,
these will be appended to the end of the list.

```pycon
>>> kvl = KeyValueList([('red', 'rose'), ('blue', 'sea'),
... ('blue', 'sky')])
>>> kvl.put('blue', 'jeans')
>>> kvl
KeyValueList([('red', 'rose'), ('blue', 'jeans')])
>>> kvl.put('red', 'heart', 'berry')
>>> kvl
KeyValueList([('red', 'heart'), ('blue', 'jeans'), ('red', 'berry')])
```

methods.name: interchange.collections.KeyValueList.remove
documentation:
Remove all items from this list that contain the key specified. If
the key is not found, a ValueError is raised.

```pycon
>>> kvl = KeyValueList([('red', 'rose'), ('blue', 'sea'),
... ('green', 'grass'), ('blue', 'sky')])
>>> kvl.remove('blue')
>>> kvl
KeyValueList([('red', 'rose'), ('green', 'grass')])
>>> kvl.remove('yellow')
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in ?
ValueError: Key 'yellow' not in list
```

methods.name: interchange.collections.KeyValueList.pop
documentation:
Remove the item at the index specified and return it. If no index
is specified, the last item is popped. If the index is out of range, an
IndexError is raised.

```pycon
>>> kvl = KeyValueList([('red', 'rose'), ('blue', 'sea'),
... ('green', 'grass'), ('blue', 'sky')])
>>> kvl.pop(2)
('green', 'grass')
>>> kvl.pop()
('blue', 'sky')
>>> kvl.pop(6)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in ?
IndexError: pop index out of range
```

methods.name: interchange.collections.KeyValueList.clear
documentation:
Remove all items from this list.

```pycon
>>> kvl = KeyValueList([('red', 'rose'), ('blue', 'sea'),
... ('green', 'grass'), ('blue', 'sky')])
>>> len(kvl)
4
>>> kvl.clear()
>>> len(kvl)
0
```

methods.name: interchange.collections.KeyValueList.sort
documentation:
Sort the items in this list into ascending order.

```pycon
>>> kvl = KeyValueList([('red', 'rose'), ('blue', 'sea'),
... ('green', 'grass'), ('blue', 'sky')])
>>> for item in kvl:
...     print(item)
('red', 'rose')
('blue', 'sea')
('green', 'grass')
('blue', 'sky')
>>> kvl.sort()
>>> for item in kvl:
...     print(item)
('blue', 'sea')
('blue', 'sky')
('green', 'grass')
('red', 'rose')
```

methods.name: interchange.collections.KeyValueList.reverse
documentation:
Reverse the order of items in this list.

```pycon
>>> kvl = KeyValueList([('red', 'rose'), ('blue', 'sea'),
... ('green', 'grass'), ('blue', 'sky')])
>>> for item in kvl:
...     print(item)
('red', 'rose')
('blue', 'sea')
('green', 'grass')
('blue', 'sky')
>>> kvl.reverse()
>>> for item in kvl:
...     print(item)
('blue', 'sky')
('green', 'grass')
('blue', 'sea')
('red', 'rose')
```

methods.name: interchange.collections.KeyValueList.copy
documentation:
Create and return a shallow copy of this list instance.

```pycon
>>> kvl = KeyValueList([('red', 'rose'), ('blue', 'sea')])
>>> kvl
KeyValueList([('red', 'rose'), ('blue', 'sea')])
>>> kvl2 = kvl.copy()
>>> kvl2.append('green', 'grass')
>>> kvl
KeyValueList([('red', 'rose'), ('blue', 'sea')])
>>> kvl2
KeyValueList([('red', 'rose'), ('blue', 'sea'), ('green', 'grass')])
```

methods.name: interchange.collections.KeyValueList.iterkeys
documentation:
Iterate through the keys in this list. If `collect` is True,
yield only unique keys.

```pycon
>>> kvl = KeyValueList([('red', 'rose'), ('blue', 'sea'),
... ('green', 'grass'), ('blue', 'sky')])
>>> for key in kvl.iterkeys():
...     print(key)
red
blue
green
blue
>>> for key in kvl.iterkeys(collect=True):
...     print(key)
red
blue
green
```

methods.name: interchange.collections.KeyValueList.itervalues
documentation:
Iterate through the values in this list. If `collect` is True,
yield a list of values for each unique key.

```pycon
>>> kvl = KeyValueList([('red', 'rose'), ('blue', 'sea'),
... ('green', 'grass'), ('blue', 'sky')])
>>> for key in kvl.itervalues():
...     print(key)
rose
sea
grass
sky
>>> for key in kvl.itervalues(collect=True):
...     print(key)
['rose']
['sea', 'sky']
['grass']
```

methods.name: interchange.collections.KeyValueList.iteritems
documentation:
Iterate through the items in this list. If `collect` is True,
yield only one item for each unique key, each with a list of
associated values.

```pycon
>>> kvl = KeyValueList([('red', 'rose'), ('blue', 'sea'),
... ('green', 'grass'), ('blue', 'sky')])
>>> for key in kvl.iteritems():
...     print(key)
('red', 'rose')
('blue', 'sea')
('green', 'grass')
('blue', 'sky')
>>> for key in kvl.iteritems(collect=True):
...     print(key)
('red', ['rose'])
('blue', ['sea', 'sky'])
('green', ['grass'])
```

A KeyValueList is a list of key-value pairs that functions as an
ordered dictionary with support for duplicate keys.

An instance can be created using either an iterable sequence of pairs or a
mapping plus an optional set of keyword arguments:

```pycon
>>> kvl = KeyValueList([('a', 1), ('c', 7), 'cx', ('b', [8, 9])], c='b')
>>> kvl
KeyValueList([('a', 1), ('c', 7), ('c', 'x'), ('b', [8, 9]), ('c', 'b')])
```

This creates a list of values that have both an associated key and a
positional index:

| index | key | value  |
| ----- | --- | ------ |
| 0     | 'a' | 1      |
| 1     | 'c' | 7      |
| 2     | 'c' | 1      |
| 3     | 'b' | [8, 9] |
| 4     | 'c' | 'b'    |

classes.name: interchange.time.Clock

methods.name: interchange.time.Clock.utc_time
documentation: Baseline implementation of current clock time.

methods.name: interchange.time.Clock.local_time
documentation:
Read and return the current local time from this clock, measured
relative to the Unix Epoch.

methods.name: interchange.time.Clock.seconds
documentation: Number of whole seconds.

A count of `seconds` and `nanoseconds`, used to denote a moment
in time relative to an externally-specified epoch.

The `seconds` and `nanoseconds` values provided to the constructor can
can have any sign but will be normalized internally into a positive or
negative `seconds` value along with a positive `nanoseconds` value
between `0` and `999,999,999` inclusive. Therefore `Clock(-1, -1)` is
normalized to `Clock(-2, 999999999)`.

Note that the structure of a {class}`.Clock` object is similar to
the `timespec` struct in C.

> ```pycon
> >>> Clock.local_time()                                              # doctest: +SKIP
> Clock(seconds=1525265942, nanoseconds=506844026)
> ```

A {class}`.Duration` object...

i64:i64:i64:i32

methods.name: interchange.time.Date.__new__
documentation: Allocate a new Date object.

methods.name: interchange.time.Date.__init__
documentation:
Initialize a Date object

As part of this construction, the day of the month is coerced
to an internal value that may or may not match the "public"
value.

With the exception of the last three days of every month, all
days are stored as-is. The last three days are instead stored
as -1 (the last), -2 (second to last) and -3 (third to last).

Therefore, for a 28-day month, the last week is as follows:

> Day | 22 23 24 25 26 27 28
> Value | 22 23 24 25 -3 -2 -1

For a 29-day month, the last week is as follows:

> Day | 23 24 25 26 27 28 29
> Value | 23 24 25 26 -3 -2 -1

For a 30-day month, the last week is as follows:

> Day | 24 25 26 27 28 29 30
> Value | 24 25 26 27 -3 -2 -1

For a 31-day month, the last week is as follows:

> Day | 25 26 27 28 29 30 31
> Value | 25 26 27 28 -3 -2 -1

This slightly unintuitive system makes some temporal arithmetic
produce a more desirable outcome.

```
param year



param month



param day



return

```

methods.name: interchange.time.Date.today
documentation: CLASS METHODS #

methods.name: interchange.time.Date.from_ordinal
documentation:
Return the {class}`.Date` that corresponds to the proleptic
Gregorian ordinal, where `0001-01-01` has ordinal 1 and
`9999-12-31` has ordinal 3,652,059. Values outside of this
range trigger a {exc}`ValueError`. The corresponding instance
method for the reverse date-to-ordinal transformation is
{meth}`.to_ordinal`.

methods.name: interchange.time.Date.parse
documentation:
Parse a string to produce a {class}`.Date`.

Accepted formats:

: 'YYYY-MM-DD'

```
param s



return

```

methods.name: interchange.time.DateTime.to_native
documentation: Convert to a native Python `datetime.datetime` value.

Regular construction of a {class}`.DateTime` object requires at
least the `year`, `month` and `day` arguments to be supplied. The
optional `hour`, `minute` and `second` arguments default to zero and
`tzinfo` defaults to {const}`None`.

While `year`, `month`, `day`, `hour` and `minute` accept only {func}`int`
values, `second` can also accept a {func}`float` value. This allows
sub-second values to be passed, with up to nine decimal places of
precision held by the object within the `second` attribute.

> ```pycon
> >>> dt = DateTime(2018, 4, 30, 12, 34, 56.789123456); dt
> DateTime(2018, 4, 30, 12, 34, 56.789123456)
> >>> dt.second
> 56.789123456
> ```
